params.text:
Speaking up to keep people safe

Whistleblowing means raising a concern about wrongdoing, malpractice or a risk that harms other people or the wider public. In adult social care, it is what you do when you see something at work that could put the people you support, your colleagues, or the public in danger — and you choose to speak up rather than stay silent.

A whistleblowing concern is raised in the public interest. That means it is about protecting others, not simply sorting out a personal problem of your own. Examples in a care setting include:

  • Abuse or neglect of a person who uses the service.
  • Unsafe care, dangerous practice or staff working while unfit.
  • Medication being given, recorded or stored incorrectly.
  • Records being falsified, or incidents being covered up.
  • Fraud, theft or the misuse of a person's money.

params.alt: Speak up: see it, report it, protected

params.text:
Why it matters

The people we support are often in a position where they cannot easily raise concerns themselves. Speaking up is one of the most important ways we protect them. Every worker has a professional responsibility to act on what they see — the culture we want is one where raising a concern is welcomed and treated as a positive contribution to safe, high-quality care.
